data.title: Strategy: Confidence Is Cracking (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Shares of Strategy (MSTR) have been in freefall since early October. MSTR falling in price is not something that I'm particularly surprised by given the declines in Bitcoin (BTC-USD). I've been stressing caution through my MSTR coverage on Seeking Alpha for well over a year. Perhaps more important than the MSTR declines, Strategy's preferred stocks (STRK)(STRF)(STRD)(STRC) are now also collapsing. This directly challenges my prior thinking and in this update, I'll detail why I think the market has lost confidence in Strategy's Bitcoin flywheel.

The Preferred Stocks Are Unraveling

As of writing, each of Strategy's preferred stocks are down significantly during intraday trading. These stocks are down between 3-8%. For income-investments, these are absolutely massive moves. In recent months, I've made the case for why I felt one of Strategy's income-based products could conceivably do well when Bitcoin's price went down. In reality, the opposite has been true and I'm now forced to reassess my thinking.

While I have not been bullish either the STRK or STRD shares in the past, I have been bullish the STRF shares. To be clear, I do still hold STRF shares. But I also see some serious problems that I think warrant trimming that position. First, STRD and STRK are now trading below IPO price. Beyond that, STRF has fallen below $100 per share for the first time since June and is currently at its lowest price level since May. What this means is that every single dollar-based preferred stock that Strategy offers is currently trading below par. Since MSTR shares are also trading at a size-able discount to mNAV, it puts Strategy in a very precarious position with limited options as the company's December dividend payments due date quickly approaches.

The Problem

For those who have followed either my work or the stock itself, this shouldn't be a surprise. Strategy does not actually generate positive income from operating its software business. We have to strip out gains or losses on digital assets that were purchased years prior to get a real assessment of the company's software business operations. On a trailing twelve month basis, Strategy's adjusted operating loss is over $42 million:

Millions Q3-24 Q4-24 Q1-25 Q2-25 Q3-25
Revenue $116.1 $120.7 $111.1 $114.5 $128.7
Cost of Revenue $34.3 $34.2 $34.0 $35.8 $38.0
SG&A $68.9 $72.4 $68.1 $70.2 $68.1
R&D $33.3 $25.7 $24.4 $24.1 $22.6
Operating Income -$20.4 -$11.6 -$15.4 -$15.6 $0.0

Source: Seeking Alpha

The only reason Strategy has been able to buy Bitcoin to the degree that it has is due to the company's ability to raise capital through various instruments including convertible notes, preferred stocks, and good old-fashioned common stock dilution. Raising money to buy BTC was easy when MSTR traded at a significant premium to mNAV.

That is no longer the case and we can see in the chart above how weekly acquisitions have fallen in line with MSTR mNAV compression. Anecdotally, it is quite obvious that MSTR shareholder appetite for more dilution of the common is virtually gone judging by sentiment online. But it's important to keep in mind that 89% of the Bitcoin purchase capital raised since preferred stock ATMs launched in March has actually come from the common ATM.

Strategy can just switch to issuing more preferred stock through ATMs then? Maybe not, actually. To illustrate why, let's look at one of Strategy's recent Bitcoin acquisition filings:

I've crudely circled the nominal value versus net proceeds for each of the four preferred ATM raises during the week of November 3rd. Strategy was really only able to utilize STRF and STRC because those where the two products that it could use to generate greater proceeds from the stock issuance than the nominal value of the obligation it was creating. That was not the case for STRK or STRD, which is why those raises were so much smaller by comparison. The point is, Strategy does not want to issue $6.4 million in preferred stocks to generate just $5.5 million in proceeds. It would much rather do the opposite. Now it can't.

At the end of September, Strategy had $54.3 million in cash and equivalents. Since then, the company has raised about $318 million through its ATM programs and purchased almost $292 million in BTC with that capital. That leaves, at best, $80 million or so in cash to pay out nearly $150 million in dividend obligations before the end of the year.

Preferred Class Shares Dividend/Shr Divis Due EOY
STRK (Due Dec) 13,974,912 $2.00 $27,949,824
STRF (Due Dec) 12,676,383 $2.50 $31,690,958
STRD (Due Dec) 12,344,308 $3.06 $37,724,205
STRC (Due Nov+Dec) 29,587,063 $0.88 $51,777,360
Total Dividends Due by EOY $149,142,347

Source: Strategy, Analyst's Calculations, estimated dividend payments

To be sure, Strategy could simply not pay the STRD dividends in December; though I suspect doing so what irreparably damage the company's ability to continue raising capital should it wish to continue doing so in the future. But even if it doesn't pay STRD shareholders in December, the company would still have a dividend shortfall through the other three products.

Strategy's Next Move

There are a variety of ways Executive Chairman Michael Saylor and company could play the precarious situation that Strategy now finds itself in. But I think these three are the most straight forward:

  1. Keep raising capital through preferred stocks
  2. Sell Bitcoin to pay dividends
  3. Continue diluting common stock holders

I think option 1 is unlikely due to what I just laid out in the section above. Strategy cannot tap the preferred ATMs while all four of them are under par without severely damaging trust in his income products, in my opinion. However, if STRF recovers from current levels and re-takes par, we could see Strategy begin to lean on that instrument a bit more despite it's relatively small ATM. Option 2 is also unlikely because it goes counter to what Saylor has been saying for years. The time to sell BTC to pay the dividends was a month ago when Bitcoin was well over $110 per coin. Given the weakness in the market, Strategy selling BTC could trigger a deeper cascade lower in the asset. Which would result in bigger problems for the company. Option 3 seems like the easiest, 'kick the can down the road' path. Thus, it seems to me to be the most likely.

Closing Summary

Again, I have to stress that Strategy had already been heavily relying on MSTR dilution to fund purchases to this point. Given the market dynamics currently on display in the preferred stocks, Strategy's ability to raise through its income products going forward is likely minimal. Frankly, I think the market is actually daring the company to sell Bitcoin to pay its preferred obligations in December. Furthermore, at a basic mNAV of 0.9, one could argue it behooves the company to sell Bitcoin to buy back its common stock to close its own mNAV discount. While I highly doubt buying back MSTR is something the company is considering doing, I have to imagine there is at least some internal discussion about buying back some of the preferred stocks while they're under par to minimize the company's perpetual dividend obligations. I could certainly be wrong for being bullish STRF earlier this year. But I don't think I'm going to be wrong on continuing to avoid MSTR.

data.title: Amprius Technologies: Positive Q3 Results, But Valuation And Technical Concerns Appear (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
I'm continuing my coverage of Amprius Technologies (AMPX). I previously wrote about AMPX back in August 2025. The stock increased by an impressive 45% since then. This significantly outperformed the S&P 500's (SPY) 3% gain over the same time.

The company's Q3 2025 earnings report provides positive insights into Amprius' operating health. I will also provide updates to AMPX's valuation and technical outlooks.

Positive Q3 2025 Results

Amprius recently reported positive results for Q3 2025. The company achieved yoy revenue growth of 173% to $21.4 million, which beat analysts' estimates by $4.62 million. The revenue gains were attributed to the addition of new customers and from large orders from existing customers. Amprius shipped its lithium batteries to 159 customers. Seventy-nine of those were repeat customers, while 80 were new customers.

Geographically, 75% of AMPX's Q3 revenue came from outside the United States. Amprius' customer diversification supports steady growth even with uncertainties such as tariffs and customer delays during the government shutdown.

Amprius got a major win with a $35 million purchase order from a leading UAS manufacturer. This is a follow-up order from the same customer who placed a $15 million order earlier this year. This is just an example of the types of repeat customers that Amprius has.

The company derived 75% of revenue from the aviation market. This was led by unmanned aerial systems [UAS]. The remaining 25% of revenue came from the light electric vehicle market [LEVs].

Growth Catalysts

Amprius believes that electric aerospace is on the verge of a multiyear transformation driven by defense and commercial demand for a new era of AI-driven autonomy. This market is estimated to grow from $40 billion to $50 billion today to $80 billion by the end of the decade according to McKinsey. About 10% of that market and 10% of the drone's materials are for batteries.

Recent government actions are driving growth for the drone market. Executive orders have been written this year to promote the use of domestic drones. Another driver is proposed changes to the Beyond Visual Line of Sight Rules [BVLOS] that the U.S. Federal Aviation Authority is debating. The new rules are expected to reform outdated regulations that held innovators back while increasing sky safety. Amprius expects the new rules to unlock the use of drones in the United States.

The One Big Beautiful Bill is estimated to allocate $10 billion to defense and unmanned systems. AMPX expects to benefit from this funding as it should create more demand for the company's lithium batteries for use in unmanned systems.

Amprius has been developing a relationship with Amazon (AMZN). Amprius was chosen for the inaugural Amazon Devices Climate Tech Accelerator cohort in July 2025. Amprius advanced to the assessment phase. This involves comprehensive testing of AMPX's customer value proposition, feasibility, sustainability impact, and supply chain readiness. The company will share updates on this progress as it develops.

AMPX's customer wins demonstrate their ability to scale up to meet volume purchase orders. The company expects this to continue to increase as Amprius adds to its customer base.

Amprius has the advantage of offering the most complete available portfolio of silicon anode materials in the industry. This gives the company a competitive advantage as AMPX's batteries have high energy density, attractive charging times, safety, and range. Amprius believes that they offer unmatched performance for commercially available batteries.

Valuation

Since Amprius is not expected to be profitable for a few more years, I use the price/sales ratio for the valuation. AMPX is trading at 8.5x expected sales of $164.5 million for 2026. This is higher than the price/sales of 5.4x that AMPX was trading at back in August when I last wrote about the company. Amprius is also trading much higher than the sector median forward price/sales of 1.7.

It is possible that the market continues to award AMPX with a premium valuation due to the company's strong above-average revenue growth. AMPX is expected to grow revenue at a strong pace of 199% in 2025 and follow that up with 128% growth in 2026 according to consensus estimates. The revenue growth rate is expected to return to a double-digit pace of about 30% in 2027.

The high revenue growth rates can be achieved if Amprius continues to be awarded orders from existing customers while acquiring new ones. Continued strong government defense spending for unmanned aircraft would also provide a tailwind for Amprius' strong revenue growth.

Technical Perspective

Amprius' daily chart above shows the stock consolidating after the strong run-up in price this year. The stock pulled back from the 52-week high of $16 which was reached in mid-October. The RSI is currently in bearish territory as it moved below 50. The MACD is also bearish as the blue line crossed below the red signal line and the histogram bars are red.

This price action looks like a profit-taking pullback as the selling looks controlled in a consolidating pattern. It is not a sharp pullback or a cause for concern in my opinion.

It could be the AMPX's premium valuation keeping the stock price suppressed. However, I do think the market can continue to award the stock a premium valuation for AMPX's above-average growth. It appears that the stock just needs a profit-taking pullback to make the valuation a little more attractive before buying pressure overtakes the selling pressure.

The stock could continue to drop from here. Although, I don't think the selling will be too steep. The Q3 earnings report was positive and the company news flow also remains positive.

The next catalyst could be news of new lucrative orders from new or existing customers. Revenue estimate upgrades could be another catalyst for the stock. Revenue upgrades could help lower the forward price/sales ratio. Another catalyst could be a well-defined technical bottom for the stock. The bottom and a new upward trend would be indicated by the blue MACD line crossing back above the red signal line while the histogram bars turn back to green.

The Risks for the Stock

Changing battery technology could be a risk for Amprius. For example, the use of solid state batteries is being developed for use in unmanned aircraft and other applications. Solid state batteries have an advantage over lithium-ion batteries as they are considered safer, have faster charging capabilities, have a higher energy density, and provide a longer lifespan. Solid state batteries can provide longer flight times for drones. Amprius may need to develop this technology or improve its current technology to remain competitive in future years.

Amprius Technologies remains unprofitable. There is no guarantee that the company will be able to turn a profit in the future. The company is on track to reach profitability in 2027 according to analysts' estimates. This can be achieved as Amprius gains economies of scale to drive down costs. Plus, AMPX should get a significant gross margin boost from its high revenue growth. The company did increase the gross margin from a negative (65%) in Q3 2024 to a positive 15.5% in Q3 2025. Failure to reach profitability at the expected time could result in a sell-off for the stock.

Competition remains an ongoing risk for Amprius. The company competes with SES AI (SES) who produces lithium batteries for EVs, drones, robotics, etc. AMPX also competes with QuantumScape (QS) who produces lithium batteries for robotics and other electronics. QuantumScape is also developing solid state battery technology. Other solid state battery developers include: Panasonic (OTCPK:PCRFF) (OTCPK:PCRHY), Samsung SDI (OTCPK:SSNLF), Factorial, and CATL. The solid state batteries could be mass produced in the late 2020s. These competitors could take market share away from Amprius.

Long-Term Outlook for Amprius Technologies

Amprius currently offers the market attractive and effective battery technology for use in unmanned aircraft and other applications. The company continues to get orders from existing customers while acquiring new ones. Recent U.S. government legislation should provide a tailwind for AMPX's technology.

I like Amprius' strong revenue growth rate. This should help the company reach profitability within about two years. We will have to watch how the battery technology evolves through the remainder of the decade. Amprius will need to continue to advance its battery technology to compete with new technologies like solid state batteries.

Since the valuation increased since my last article and the stock remains in a consolidation phase or pullback, I'm lowering my rating to a 'HOLD'. An improved valuation along with a better technical set-up would make me reconsider this rating.

data.title: Permian Resources: Excellent Cost And Production Performance Continues In Q3 2025
data.description:
Permian Resources (PR) reported excellent Q3 2025 results, with a 6% quarter-over-quarter increase in oil production and a 7% quarter-over-quarter increase in total production.

It also increased its full-year production guidance by 2%, which was mainly driven by continued well outperformance.

Permian's cost performance was strong, with a 6% quarter-over-quarter decrease in controllable cash costs per BOE and Q3 2025 D&C costs per lateral foot that were down -11% compared to 2024.

Permian has continued to work on natural gas marketing efforts and expects to sell most of its natural gas production into premium markets (such as the Gulf Coast) by 2027.

The strong production and cost performance, as well as the natural gas marketing efforts, have resulted in a $1 increase to my estimate of Permian's value. I now estimate its value at $18.50 per share in a long-term (after 2026) $70 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas environment.

Q3 2025 Results

Permian Resources reported excellent Q3 2025 results, reporting a 6% quarter-over-quarter increase in oil production, bringing that to 186,937 barrels of oil per day. Permian's total production increased by approximately 7% quarter-over-quarter, reaching an average of 410,225 BOEPD in Q3 2025.

While Permian has also been active with various acquisitions, I'd estimate that around 3% of its quarter-over-quarter production growth was due to acquisitions and 3% to 4% production growth was organic. Permian noted that it continued to see outperformance with its new wells.

Permian performed well with respect to costs in addition to its strong production performance. It mentioned that its Q3 2025 D&C costs were around $725 per lateral foot, down 11% from 2024 levels.

Permian's controllable cash costs per BOE decreased by 6% quarter-over-quarter, ending up at $7.36 per BOE. This was its best quarterly performance on that metric during 2025 and is towards the lower end of its full-year guidance for $7.25 to $8.25 per BOE.

Permian focused mainly on debt reduction during the quarter, reducing its debt by approximately $460 million. It repaid $287 million in 5.375% unsecured notes due 2026 at par and redeemed $170 million in 3.25% convertible notes due 2028. As the conversion price for the convertible notes was approximately $5.56, noteholders exercised their right to convert the notes into 30.6 million Class A common shares.

Permian still has related capped call transactions in place that should reduce the effective dilution from conversion. It also repurchased 2.3 million shares for $30 million during the quarter and paid its typical quarterly base dividend of $0.15 per share.

In terms of acquisitions, Permian added 5,500 net leasehold acres and 2,400 net royalty acres in Q3 2025 for $180 million in total consideration (over approximately 250 small transactions). Most of the acquired acreage was in New Mexico and is relatively lightly developed overall (with approximately 800 BOEPD in current production). This brings Permian's acquisition spending to over $800 million so far in 2025.

Natural Gas Marketing Efforts

Permian mentioned that it has firm capacity on long-distance pipelines and out of basin sales agreements that will help it sell 330 MMCF per day of natural gas at premium prices (compared to Waha) in 2026. These agreements increase to around 700 MMCF per day in 2028 natural gas volumes.

Permian estimates that this will result in a $1 per Mcf improvement in its realized prices relative to Waha in 2026, increasing its 2026 free cash flow by over $100 million.

The expected Waha differential is more modest in 2027 based on strip, so the improvement in realized prices relative to Waha may not be as high then. However, the agreements should still ensure that minimal realized natural gas prices are a thing of the past for Permian. It realized only $0.78 per Mcf for its natural gas (before hedges) during the first three quarters of 2025, and this was even lower in 2024.

Updated Guidance

As a result of its strong production performance, Permian increased its full-year 2025 production guidance for the second time. It now expects 394,000 BOEPD in 2025 production at its guidance midpoint, which is a 2% increase from its prior guidance. It also expects approximately 181,500 barrels per day in oil production, which is also a 2% increase from its prior guidance around oil production. Most of this guidance increase is from organic growth.

Permian left the rest of its full-year guidance unchanged, although it does look to be on track to end up in the lower half of its guidance around controllable cash costs per BOE.

Based on Permian's updated full-year guidance, it expects roughly 407,000 BOEPD in Q4 2025 production, including around 187,375 barrels per day of oil production. This would be roughly flat quarter-over-quarter oil production growth along with a slight -1% quarter-over-quarter decline in total production.

Notes on Valuation

I am increasing my estimate of Permian's value by $1, from $17.50 per share to $18.50 per share.

This continues to assume long-term prices (after 2026) of $70 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas.

Permian's estimated value has improved due to its continued production outperformance (mostly driven by its newer wells) and its natural gas marketing efforts.

Permian has been reducing its exposure to Waha prices and should sell most of its natural gas production in 2027 and 2028 into the Gulf Coast and Dallas-Fort Worth markets.

Around 29% of Permian's production is natural gas, and it has been hampered at times in the past by realizing close to zero for that production when Waha prices have bottomed out.

Conclusion

Permian Resources has executed very well, delivering outperformance on both operating costs and production in Q3 2025. It has also reduced its D&C costs per lateral foot by 11% compared to 2024 and increased its firm transport and sales agreements to cover most of its natural gas production by 2027. This results in most of its natural gas being sold into premium markets instead of at Waha prices (which are sometimes near zero or even negative).

I have increased my estimate of Permian's value to $18.50 per share in a scenario with current strip prices until the end of 2026 and then $70 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas after that time.

data.title: Renewed Peace Talks Weigh On Oil Prices
data.description:
By Warren Patterson, Head of Commodities Strategy and Ewa Manthey, Commodities Strategist

Reports that the US and Russia are working on a peace plan for Ukraine put downward pressure on oil and European gas prices yesterday.

Energy: Russia-Ukraine peace talk noise

Oil prices came under pressure yesterday, with ICE Brent settling about 2.1% lower. Some of the downward pressure is derived from reports that the US and Russia are working on a new peace plan for Ukraine.

However, with suggestions that the plan is favourable towards Russia, it may be unlikely that Ukraine will back it. Signs that the US is still trying to work on a deal ease some concerns over further sanctions against Russia and also how strongly current curbs will be enforced.

The Energy Information Administration (EIA) released its weekly US inventory data yesterday, reporting that US commercial crude oil inventories fell by 3.43m barrels over the week. The decline was driven by stronger crude exports, which grew 1.34m b/d week on week.

Refiners also increased run rates, leading to stronger crude oil inputs. Stronger run rates shouldn’t be too surprising, given the strength in refinery margins, while refiners are also exiting maintenance season. Changes in inventory for refined products were more bearish.

Gasoline inventories increased by 2.33m barrels, while distillate stocks edged up by 171k barrels. Implied gasoline demand was also weaker, falling by 500k b/d WoW. The more bearish gasoline numbers saw the Reformulated Blendstock for Oxygenate Blending (RBOB) crack coming under some pressure yesterday.

European gas prices also traded lower yesterday, with the Title Transfer Facility (TTF) settling almost 2.4% lower. Reports of peace talks between Russia and the US weighed on prices. However, colder weather has led to gas storage falling at a faster pace over the last few days.

EU gas storage is now 81% full, down from a peak of just over 83% in mid-October. Meanwhile, the latest positioning data shows that investment funds cut their net long in TTF by 8.7TWh to 15.6TWh, the smallest net long since March 2024.

This was driven by fresh shorts entering the market, with the gross short rising by 19TWh to 434.3TWh, yet another record gross short. It leaves the market vulnerable to a short-covering rally in the event of any supply surprises or extended cold snaps over the winter.

Metals: Chile lifts copper price forecasts

Chile, the biggest copper producer, raised its price forecasts for this year and next. The country’s copper agency, Cochilco, cited global supply disruptions, lower interest rates, a weaker dollar and global economic resilience.

Copper is expected to average $4.45 a pound this year and $4.55 in 2026, according to Cochilco’s quarterly report. It previously forecast $4.30 for both this year and next. Copper supply has been hit by a wave of disruptions this year, including an accident at the El Teniente mine in Chile in July.

Cochilco now expects no production growth in Chile for the year, down from a previous forecast of 1.5%. In 2026, Chilean production is forecast to grow 2.5% to 5.6 million metric tonnes. However, a key assumption about next year's growth is that El Teniente will operate as normal, which Codelco says is unlikely.

Agriculture: Sugar and ethanol dynamics

Sugar prices rose yesterday before giving back all of these gains. Reports that India is considering raising ethanol prices provided support, as it could mean more sugar being diverted to ethanol production, helping to eat into the large global surplus expected. Meanwhile, in Brazil, mills should also be moving to favor ethanol over sugar production, with sugar prices trading below ethanol parity.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Tracking Seth Klarman's Baupost Group Holdings - Q3 2025 Update
data.description:
This article is part of a series that provides an ongoing analysis of the changes made to Baupost Group's 13F stock portfolio on a quarterly basis. It is based on Klarman’s regulatory 13F Form filed on 11/13/2025. Please visit our Tracking Seth Klarman’s Baupost Group Holdings article for an idea of how his holdings have progressed over the years and our previous update for the fund's moves during Q2 2025.

Baupost Group’s 13F portfolio value increased from ~$4.11B to ~$4.76B this quarter. The total number of 13F securities decreased from 20 to 19. The portfolio is heavily concentrated with Restaurant Brands, Alphabet, Elevance Health, CRH plc, and Willis Towers Watson together accounting for ~46% of the 13F holdings.

Since its inception (1982), Baupost Group’s 13F portfolio has accounted for between 2.4% and 15% of the Assets Under Management (AUM). The current allocation is at the high end of that range. The rest of the AUM is diversified among cash, debt, real estate, and hedges. On average, the fund has held ~25% cash over the last decade. Seth Klarman’s distinct investment style is elaborated in his 1991 book, “Margin of Safety: Risk-averse value investing strategies for the thoughtful investor.” The book is out-of-print, and copies sell for a huge premium.

New Stakes:

Union Pacific (UNP): UNP is a large 7.43% of the portfolio position purchased this quarter at prices between ~$211 and ~$241, and the stock currently trades at ~$221.

Genuine Parts (GPC): The ~4% GPC stake was established this quarter at prices between ~$120 and ~$144. The stock is now at ~$125.

Americold Realty Trust (COLD): COLD is a very small 0.93% of the portfolio position purchased this quarter at prices between ~$12 and ~$17. The stock is now trading below that range at $10.40.

Stake Disposals:

Liberty Broadband (LBRDK): The 1.79% LBRDK stake was built during the last quarter at prices between ~$71 and ~$104. The disposal this quarter was at prices between ~$58 and ~$102. The stock currently trades at $44.88.

Viasat (VSAT): VSAT was 3.25% of the portfolio position. Klarman first purchased VSAT in 2008 at a cost basis in the high teens. In July 2020, Baupost participated in ViaSat’s 4.47M share private placement by acquiring ~2.56M shares at ~$39 per share. Q3 2024 saw ~11% selling at prices between $11.94 and $24.49. That was followed by a ~17% reduction during Q1 2025 at prices between $7.45 and $12.55. The position was sold this quarter at prices between ~$14 and ~$34. The stock currently trades at $34.92.

ICON plc (ICLR): ICLR was 1.43% of the portfolio position purchased during Q1 2025 at prices between ~$168 and ~$228. The elimination this quarter was at prices between ~$139 and ~$211. The stock is now at ~$159.

Amcor plc (AMCR): The 1.22% AMCR stake was established during the last quarter at prices between $8.37 and $9.82. It was sold this quarter at prices between $7.90 and $10. It is now at $8.42.

Stake Increases:

Restaurant Brands (QSR): The 11.11% QSR stake is now their largest 13F position. It was established during Q4 2024 at prices between ~$65 and ~$73. There was a one-third stake increase during the next quarter at prices between ~$60 and ~$70. This quarter saw the position more than double at prices between ~$61 and ~$72. The stock is now at $67.83.

Elevance Health (ELV): The ~9% ELV stake was established during Q1 2025 at prices between ~$365 and ~$439. The last quarter saw a ~150% stake increase at prices between ~$357 and ~$459. That was followed by an ~115% stake increase this quarter at prices between ~$274 and ~$401. The stock is now at ~$316.

Ferguson Enterprises (FERG): FERG is a fairly large 5.45% of the portfolio position purchased during Q4 2024 at prices between ~$174 and ~$222. The stock currently trades at ~$237. There was a minor ~2% stake increase this quarter.

Eagle Materials (EXP): EXP is a 4.60% of the portfolio stake established during Q1 2024 at prices between ~$196 and ~$272. The next quarter saw a ~30% stake increase at prices between ~$213 and ~$274. Q3 2024 saw a ~45% stake increase at prices between ~$212 and ~$292. That was followed by a ~28% stake increase during the next quarter at prices between $246.48 and $315. Q1 2025 saw a ~43% further increase at prices between ~$205 and ~$265. There was a ~25% selling during the last quarter at prices between ~$192 and ~$243. This quarter saw a ~40% stake increase at prices between ~$200 and ~$243. The stock currently trades at ~$204.

Stake Decreases:

Alphabet Inc. (GOOGL): The 9.50% of the portfolio GOOGL stake is currently the second largest 13F position. It was purchased during Q1 2020 at prices between ~$53 and ~$76. The position has wavered. Recent activity follows. Q2 2024 saw ~65% selling at prices between ~$152 and ~$187. There was a ~37% stake increase during the next quarter at prices between ~$150 and ~$192. That was followed by a ~46% stake increase during Q1 2025 at prices between ~$152 and ~$209. The last quarter saw a ~27% further increase at prices between ~$143 and ~$183. There was a ~30% selling this quarter at prices between ~$173 and ~$256. The stock is now at ~$293.

CRH plc (CRH): The 8.52% CRH stake was primarily built during Q3 2023 at prices between ~$53 and ~$60. The stake was decreased by 19% during Q1 2024 at prices between ~$66 and ~$87. The next quarter saw a ~55% stake increase at prices between ~$73 and ~$86. Q3 2024 saw a ~38% reduction at prices between ~$72 and ~$93. There was a ~42% stake increase during the last quarter at prices between ~$77 and ~$100. This quarter saw a ~12% trimming at prices between ~$91 and ~$122. The stock currently trades at ~$110.

Willis Towers Watson (WTW): WTW is currently at 7.90% of the portfolio. It was established during Q1 2021 at prices between ~$200 and ~$235. The stake has wavered. Recent activity follows. There was a ~17% selling during Q1 2025 at prices between ~$303 and ~$344. That was followed by a ~14% reduction during the last quarter at prices between ~$293 and ~$341. This quarter also saw ~17% selling at prices between ~$298 and ~$346. The stock currently trades at ~$317.

WESCO International (WCC): The 6.67% WCC position was purchased during Q1 2024 at prices between ~$133 and ~$194. The next quarter saw the stake built at prices between ~$152 and ~$191. There was a ~55% stake increase during Q3 2024 at prices between ~$149 and ~$183. That was followed by a ~14% stake increase during the next quarter at prices between $163.76 and $212.73. Q1 2025 saw another ~40% stake increase at prices between ~$149 and ~$203. There was a one-third reduction this quarter at prices between ~$183 and ~$228. The stock is now at ~$252.

Liberty Global (LBTYK): LBTYK is currently at 6.56% of the portfolio. It was established during Q3 2018 at prices between ~$15 and ~$19 and increased by ~120% in the next quarter at prices between ~$10 and ~$18. There was another ~27% stake increase in Q1 2019 at prices between ~$10 and ~$16. Q1 2025 saw one-third selling at prices between ~$11 and ~$14. That was followed by a ~10% trimming this quarter at prices between $9.95 and $12.32. The stock currently trades at $11.02.

Note: The prices quoted above are adjusted for the Sunrise (OTC:SNREY) spinoff in November 2024.

Kept Steady:

Dollar General (DG): DG is 5.79% of the portfolio position purchased during Q3 2024 at prices between ~$80 and ~$132, and the stock currently trades at ~$100. There was an ~11% trimming during Q4 2024 at prices between ~$73 and ~$83. The last quarter saw a ~27% stake increase at prices between ~$85 and ~$111.

Fidelity National Information Services (FIS): FIS is 5.25% of the portfolio position purchased during Q1 2025 at prices between ~$67 and ~$84, and the stock currently trades below that range at $62.66. There was an ~9% stake increase during the last quarter.

GDS Holdings (GDS): The 2.64% GDS stake saw a ~65% stake increase during Q2 2024 at prices between ~$6 and ~$10. There was a ~24% selling during the last quarter at prices between ~$17 and ~$31. The stock currently trades at $29.75.

Fiserv, Inc. (FISV): FISV is 2.42% of the portfolio position purchased during the last quarter at prices between ~$156 and ~$227, and the stock currently trades well below that range at $61.17.

Herbalife (HLF): The 1.38% HLF position was increased by 34% during Q1 2024 at prices between $8.03 and $15.36. That was followed by a ~130% stake increase during the next quarter at prices between $7.65 and $11.70. Q4 2024 saw another ~50% stake increase at prices between $6.33 and $8.67. There was a ~225% further increase during the next quarter at prices between $5.04 and $9.23. The stock is now at $9.22.

PagSeguro Digital (PAGS): PAGS is a small 0.52% of the portfolio position purchased during the last quarter at prices between $7.36 and $10.21, and it now goes for $9.91.

Tamboran Resources (TBN): The very small 0.33% TBN stake was kept steady this quarter.

The spreadsheet below highlights changes to Klarman's 13F stock holdings in Q3 2025:

Source: John Vincent. Data constructed from Baupost Group’s 13F filings for Q2 2025 and Q3 2025.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Arista Networks: Supply Constraints Limit Upside (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Back in September, I provided an update for Arista Networks Inc. (ANET). In that article, I reiterated my sell rating and expressed my view that a reality check could be coming as the outlook was soft and the valuation was rich. As you can see below, the stock has taken quite a tumble recently after earnings on Nov 4th, and so my thesis seems to have played out like I envisioned. Today, let's see if my rating needs updating after these events.

In the below analysis, it is shown that Arista Networks is overall well positioned as the data center switching market continues to expand. From their purchase commitments, you will see that demand isn't the problem. Instead, it seems that supply is dragging on growth, and forward guidance suggests these issues will persist into 2026. That said, the valuation has corrected, and so the stock now seems to be trading back at a reasonable valuation with regard to the long-term growth outlook. As a result, I'm upgrading to a hold.

Long Term Growth Story Is Intact

Firstly, despite my bearish take on the stock in my previous article, I would say that the company's long-term growth story remains highly intact. Even though there are recent worries about tech valuations, there is nothing to suggest that the trajectory of infrastructure buildout will slow. In the above charts, you can see the opportunity at hand.

For data center Ethernet switching, you can see that we are still in the very early innings, and you can see that the market could potentially hit $50 billion by 2029, a significant expansion from today's ~$30 billion market. As for campus Ethernet switching, forecasts also indicate notable expansion of the market from 2025 onwards for clients of all sizes, and so overall, from these two charts, you can see that Arista Networks is likely to continue benefitting from tailwinds in the next few years.

The above chart is a bit different and shows the data center ethernet switch market potential when excluding AI back-end networks. As you can see, moving forward, 800 Gbps switches will lead the way in terms of growth. Beyond that, 1.6 Tbps will become the bleeding edge. Therefore, in order to capture the growth of the data center switching market as a whole, Arista Networks will have to keep themselves at the forefront of the industry. So far, it seems they are succeeding.

Near the end of October, the company unveiled the new 800G R4 Series platform. Seamus Crehan, President of Crehan Research stated that "the 800GbE market is growing explosively, with port shipments more than tripling sequentially in Q2 '25, and Arista led in branded market share for both 800GbE as well as overall data center Ethernet switching." Therefore, it seems that Arista Networks currently holds both a technological and market share edge. Both factors should help to position the company well as infrastructure trends continue. Now let's take a look at their financial results for Q3.

Demand Is Strong

For Q3, Arista Networks reported revenues of $2.308 billion, up 27.5% YoY. While that's still a quite strong growth rate, it does represent a deceleration from the 30.4% increase seen in Q2. Even though this is definitely undesirable, it doesn't necessarily mean that demand for their products has weakened.

In the third quarter, the company recorded purchase commitments of $4.8 billion, double that of the prior year period, showing off just how strong demand is. The company stated that "purchase commitments remain elevated due to new product introductions," and so it should be clear that their technological leadership remains their competitive advantage.

Furthermore, it is reported that inventory turns improved from 1.3 to 1.4 YoY, and so clearly their products are not stuck on the shelf. At the end of the day, Arista Networks was able to beat analyst estimates for revenue by a fairly decent margin of $41.76 million, and so I would still consider their top-line results to be respectable.

Other Key Metrics

Strong Gross Margin

I discussed margins and profitability in my previous article as well, but for Q3, YoY gross margin expansion was even stronger than back in Q2. There was a 20 basis point expansion in the previous quarter, but for Q3, they managed a 60 basis point improvement. This suggests even better cost control and perhaps even more pricing power. Considering that demand is strong, as shown above, it wouldn't be surprising for Arista Networks to hold significant pricing power due to the technological competitiveness of their products.

Operating Margin Dipped

Despite good news for gross margin, operating margin dipped 50 basis points to 48.6%. In Q2 they saw notable expansion. However, investors shouldn't be too concerned at this point. Arista Networks stated that this weakness was "due to increased new product introduction costs" and so really there isn't anything wrong with the company's operations. It was mentioned above that new products are driving purchase commitments, and so these expenses are necessary for the long-term growth of the company.

Bottom Line Growth Also Slows

With operating margin coming in a bit weak, it shouldn't be a major surprise that bottom line growth underperformed when compared to top line growth. For EPS, Arista Networks reported an adjusted figure of $0.75, representing a 25% increase YoY. While they were still able to beat estimates by $0.04, there was a significant growth slowdown from Q2. Furthermore, according to consensus expectations, further slowdown is likely for the next few quarters, and so it is possible that some profitability weakness will continue. We'll take a look at their guidance next.

Near Term Outlook

For the fourth quarter, Arista Networks is expecting to see revenues of $2.35 billion at the midpoint, which means YoY growth of 21.8%. That would be another deceleration of top line growth from Q3. While demand is not a major concern at this stage according to the purchase commitments, further deceleration could start to be worrying as it may show that they aren't meeting demand in an efficient manner. For gross margin, they reported a non-GAAP figure of 64.2% in the year-ago quarter, and so cost control may weaken. However, in a reversal of fortunes, operating margin may shift back to expansion, as 47.0% was reported in 2024 Q4. Therefore, the increase in new product introduction expenses discussed earlier likely won't carry over.

A Softer 2026 Expected

In the EPS consensus expectations chart shown earlier, bottom-line growth was projected to continue moderating. That is due to three key factors, as shown by their preliminary 2026 guidance. For revenue growth, they expect just 20%, a slowdown from the expected full-year 2025 growth rate of between 26% and 27%. This either shows that demand will level off a bit or the company may struggle to keep up with demand. The latter seems more likely, but either way, continued slowdown could be a concern.

In addition to that, Arista Networks expects gross margin contraction at the midpoint due to "customer mix" and operating margin contraction due to continued investments in the business. While investments are not inherently a bad thing, they project a 400 basis point contraction from 2025 levels at the midpoint, and so the magnitude of deterioration may be a concern. As a whole, while purchase commitments are reassuring for demand, moving forward, the outlook seems a bit shaky in my view.

Valuation Slides

The forward P/E has corrected from 53.35 in my previous article to 43.00 and that potentially improves the risk/reward of the stock in my view. Firstly, for a good part of today's article, we discussed how the market is projected to expand in the next few years and how Arista Networks is well positioned. I keep going back to the purchase commitments figure because it shows that the company's growth is getting constrained by supply rather than the lack of demand. Therefore, despite the softer than desired Q4 and 2026 guidance, I would say that the company remains a robust business at its core.

I believe the sharp recent contraction in the valuation is a good reality check in that the near term isn't looking too rosy and now the stock is likely back to a fair valuation that accounts for the long term growth story. While the supply chain is not overly under their control, management should make this a key focus moving forward if they want to reaccelerate their financials.

Takeaway

Arista Networks is a leader that's well-positioned in the market. But at the same time, metrics point to supply constraints as their growth-limiting factor. With supply chains being so complex, there likely isn't an easy fix. The valuation has compensated, however, with the forward P/E ratio showing rather sharp contraction as of late. At this point, I view the valuation as a good demonstration of long-term opportunity along with near-term challenges, and so I have decided to upgrade to a hold rating.

data.title: Circle Internet Group: Share Price Now More Reflective Of Mounting Risks (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Circle's (CRCL) third-quarter results were strong, with efforts to diversify revenue beginning to pay off. This will only play out slowly, though, and in the meantime there is little to prevent a complete implosion of Circle's business in the event of a recession.

I previously highlighted the enormous risks Circle was facing, and its share price is down over 40% since then. Short-term interest rates continue to fall, although this has been fairly modest so far. Bitcoin's price is also down materially in recent weeks, which could temper demand for stablecoins. Despite this, I am now more neutral on Circle's stock after the recent decline, as the longer term narrative remains fairly compelling. Having said that, there will little to support Circle's share price if macro conditions continue to deteriorate.

Market Conditions

The most important recent development for stablecoins is the introduction of a far more favorable regulatory environment. While the full impact likely won't be felt for some time, Circle is best positioned to capitalize on this situation due to its focus on compliance.

Stablecoins in circulation increased 59% YoY in Q3, supported by surging markets. Circle’s market share also increased to 29% in the quarter. Stablecoin transaction volumes were reportedly up around 130% YoY, with USDC’s share growing to 40%. While this is positive, the recent market pullback is likely to temper demand for stablecoins in the short term. If this situation evolves into a bear market or recession, USDC's market capitalization could drop significantly, despite secular tailwinds.

Circle Business Updates

Circle's business continues to be dominated by the issuance of USDC, a US-dollar-denominated stablecoin. Circle also now offers the EURC stablecoin, but its market cap is still negligible. This is important as it means that Circle's revenue is predominantly dependent on short-term interest rates.

Circle is building financial infrastructure that extends well beyond just USDC, though. This includes the Arc layer-1 blockchain, which is tailored for stablecoins. Circle recently launched the Arc public testnet with more than 100 participants. While this is an interesting strategic move, it is not guaranteed to be successful. Arc faces fierce competition, and it is a late entrant into the market. This must be weighed against the fact that Circle has a distribution advantage and could use incentives to drive adoption. For example, Circle is already considering introducing a native token on the Arc Network.

Circle also offers CCTP (Cross-Chain Transfer Protocol), an infrastructure solution that enables the movement of USDC across blockchains, improving liquidity and the user experience. CCTP volume reached 31.3 billion USD in Q3, up 640% YoY. Circle has suggested that this represented around 47% of the bridged volumes that it tracks.

Circle also has the Circle Payments Network, a stablecoin payments solution targeted at financial institutions. It abstracts the complexity of the underlying blockchain system and enables fast, low-cost, and compliant global payments. CPN was only launched in May 2025 but is already seeing significant transaction volume growth. Support for 5 new chains was added in the quarter, and Circle continues to expand the CPN product portfolio. This includes CPN Console, a self-service solution for CPN members, and CPN Payouts, which enables automated stablecoin payouts on CPN. There are now 29 network members and a pipeline of 500 financial institutions waiting to join. CPN transaction volume is currently at around a 3.4 billion USD annual run rate.

The other important part of Circle's business at the moment is USYC, a tokenized money market fund. Circle acquired this product to support the rising use of yield-bearing digital assets as collateral in margin trading. USYC is now the second largest TMMF globally, with roughly 1 billion USD AUM.

Financial Analysis

USDC in circulation reached almost 74 billion USD in the third quarter, an increase of 108% YoY. Onchain transactions using USDC also grew 580% YoY to 9.6 trillion USD. Revenue and reserve income was only up 66% to 740 million USD though, with lower interest rates offsetting a substantial portion of the volume increase (return on reserves of ~4.15%). Transaction revenue totaled 4.7 million USD in the quarter. Other revenue was 29 million USD in Q3, up from less than 1 million USD in the prior year's comparable quarter. Subscription and services revenue totaled 23.6 million USD, which primarily relates to blockchain network partnerships.

Circle hasn't given specific near-term revenue guidance. USDC in circulation is expected to grow at a 40% CAGR over a multi-year period, though, which probably puts revenue growth at less than a 30% CAGR. Circle also now expects 90-100 million USD Other Revenue in 2025, which would mean that Other Revenue is fairly flat sequentially. Given the underlying trends in the business, this guidance appears overly conservative.

Total distribution and transaction and other costs increased 74% YoY in Q3 to 448 million USD. The increase was driven by distribution incentives and growth of USDC balances held on Coinbase's platform. As a result, Circle's RLDC margin declined to 39.5%.

Circle's adjusted operating expenses only increased 35% YoY to 131 million USD though, with adjusted EBITDA reaching 166 million USD at a 22% margin. While Circle has a highly profitable business, this would soon change if interest rates were to drop significantly.

Conclusion

I remain skeptical of Circle's business due to its current dependence on interest rates and cryptocurrency speculation. The company is moving in the right direction, though, with its infrastructure solutions and tokenized money market fund continuing to grow rapidly. It will likely be years before these alternative revenue sources are large enough to justify Circle's current market capitalization. In the meantime, weakening economic conditions continue to present a serious threat to Circle's share price. Despite this, I am now more neutral on Circle's stock, as it could soon find support after the recent decline.

data.title: McDonald's: A Resilient Defensive Play, But Premium Valuation May Cap Upside
data.description:
Following my last article on McDonald's (MCD), the stock price appreciated by 0.50%, yet underperformed the benchmark. This highlights my anticipation, following the thesis of an elevated multiple capping the upside in the near term.

My outlook hasn't changed. Although McDonald's remains a solid defensive play, which could help withstand market turbulence, MCD trades at a significant premium, clouding the future outlook. The forward P/E of 25x indicates a 55% premium versus the sector median of 16x and roughly 8% overvaluation compared to the average company in the S&P 500 at 23x earnings multiple. The iconic company deserves a premium multiple, but the current one may appear too inflated.

That's why I rate McDonald's a Hold. I still think the company may achieve the $329 price target over the next 12 months, indicating a 7% upside possibility, but it is unlikely to outperform the benchmark. I believe MCD is likely to follow bottom-line growth.

FQ3 2025: Double Miss Raises Red Flags

On November 5, the restaurant chain company delivered FQ3 2025 results. McDonald's posted a double-miss quarterly report, which raises red flags and indicates weakness in consumer spending. The market reacted to the results with a 2% decline over the following trading day.

McDonald's posted $7.08 billion in revenue, a 3% increase on a year-over-year basis, but $11 million below analysts' expectations. The bottom line arrived at $3.22 in diluted EPS, roughly flat on a year-over-year basis versus $3.23 in the same quarter last year, but $0.11 below what the market had anticipated. The company continues steady top-line growth, but deceleration in bottom-line growth may appear concerning, especially when MCD trades at a premium valuation to both the industry and the benchmark.

In the earnings release, McDonald's highlighted a positive tailwind, indicating steady operations across same-restaurant sales. The US market experienced a 2.4% increase on a year-over-year basis, mainly driven by positive check growth. The international markets surged by 4.3% over the past 12 months, primarily due to increased demand in Germany and Australia.

The international developmental licensed markets rose by 4.7% on a year-over-year basis, all geographic regions delivering positive growth, but led by comparable sales growth in Japan. I think this is important, indicating solid momentum in demand for the iconic brand and a significant market position globally, implying geographically well-diversified business operations.

I would also like to emphasize that franchised restaurants have led the top-line growth, suggesting a 7% increase on a year-over-year basis. What I think may be concerning is that sales by the company's owned and operated restaurants have decreased by 3%, arguing a weakening consumer and limited spending budgets. This may be concerning for long-term investors, and if comparable sales continue to erode, this could indicate a more structural issue for the company. I think investors should monitor dynamics further.

The company delivered $3.36 billion in operating income, a 5% increase on a year-over-year basis, and $2.28 billion in net income, reflecting a 1% growth over the past 12 months. These results support my bullish stance, representing a strong and mature business, but also struggling to accelerate revenue growth. Due to this, I argue that the market may not be willing to pay a higher premium for the company's stock.

To conclude, the company continues maintaining a dominant stance in the fast food industry, yet top and bottom line weakness argues for existing headwinds. I am favoring uncertainty around the macroeconomic outlook and weakening US consumer, rather than any structural issues, but investors should monitor the company's performance dynamics. If the economy enters a recession, sentiment could significantly shift, and the stock could correct from a premium valuation.

On the other hand, McDonald's remains well-diversified globally, including both the company's owned restaurants and licensed franchises. The company experiences steady sales growth in Germany, Australia, and Japan, which signals business continuation, yet I am not sure whether a solid low-single-digit revenue growth could be sufficient to justify multiple expansion.

Premium Valuation for Premium Business

I kindly invite you to read my previous article, which provides a more in-depth analysis of McDonald's valuations. However, I would also like to emphasize a few key things that support my thesis.

The chart below suggests that the current forward P/E of approximately 25 indicates a higher trading range over the past few years. This supports the thesis of limited upside, suggesting that investors are willing to pay a premium for robust business, yet I am not sure if stagnating performance supports the positive outlook.

One argument could be that MCD offers a defensive approach. The broader stock market has rallied since the April lows, and once volatility returns to the markets, McDonald's may appear to be a compelling opportunity to diversify into. However, the current valuation leaves little room for error, and that supports my neutral stance.

In addition to this, the forward EV/Sales and Price/Sales ratios of 10.20 and 8.20, respectively, argue for a bearish narrative, and that is another concern to account for. These valuation metrics imply an 8x overvaluation versus the sector medians of 1.29 and 0.90, which highlights that the market has likely already priced in the future revenue growth. At the current valuation ratios, the market knows that MCD is a stable and resilient business, but I don't think there is more room for multiple expansion. Even if the revenue growth resumes, these metrics argue that the stock price is not likely to appreciate, and it may need to unlock a new growth cycle. This stands for consolidation, which would result in the stock price mirroring the bottom-line performance.

The chart above indicates that MCD is used to trading at an elevated forward EV/Revenue level, but it also states a higher end of the trading range, signaling consolidation and capped upside in the near term.

McDonald's operates at substantial margins, and that is supporting a bullish narrative. The net income margin (TTM) of 32% suggests a 7x outperformance versus peers at 4.42%. The gross profit margin (TTM) of 57% reveals a 50% higher valuation versus the sector median of 38%. Although this argues for a bullish continuation, the company is heavily leveraged with nearly $56 billion in total obligations. Over the past 12 months, MCD paid $1.55 billion in interest expenses to service the debt, and that offsets the bullish outlook.

I kindly invite you to read my previous article, which indicates a more detailed valuation analysis. Nevertheless, McDonald's presents a mixed picture. The company is facing significant top and bottom line headwinds, but due to its iconic brand and mature business, this is likely a temporary hiccup. In the meantime, international business offsets the clouded comparable sales, and once the US consumer strengthens, top-line growth is likely to resume at a mid-single-digit pace.

MCD continues operating at substantial margins, which is supporting a bullish outlook, but it remains a heavily leveraged business with a significant financial burden. In addition to this, the company is not cheap, and valuation metrics indicate that even if the revenue growth resumes, the market may have already priced in the stock price, which may limit the upside in the near term. This supports my neutral stance on the restaurant chain company.

Wall Street estimates $12.14 in diluted EPS for FY2025 and $13.23 in diluted EPS for FY2026. I believe the company may be able to achieve these targets, supported by a strong presence in the operating fields, mature business flow, and significant margins. I also think that the stock is likely to mirror bottom-line growth, but I don't think MCD is well-positioned to outperform the benchmark. If we apply the current earnings multiple of 25x to the FY2026 EPS of $13.23, we arrive at my price target of $329.

Unlikely to Outperform the Benchmark

I think McDonald's may appear to be a compelling opportunity for long-term investors seeking to diversify in the mature business, operating at an appealing margin, and having a significant presence in the space. The company also offers a 2.4% dividend yield, which may come as an attractive bonus to a defensive approach. MCD remains a stable and resilient business.

However, the risks and concerns remain, too, as I highlighted above and in my previous article. The company's premium valuation clouds the outlook, alongside stagnating top and bottom line growth, which could decline if the economy enters a recession. McDonald's remains heavily leveraged and operates in an industry full of fierce competition. If consumer habits change or people turn to healthier alternatives, structural issues may appear.

The clouded narrative supports my neutral stance. I rate McDonald's a Hold with a $329 price target. This argues for a 7% upside possibility without dividends but is unlikely to outperform the benchmark. 37 Wall Street analysts forecast a $331 price target, offering an 8% upside potential, and that is roughly in line with my estimate.

data.title: BUG: Increasing Exposure (Upgrade To Buy)
data.description:
Introduction

I have been a fan of the Cybersecurity sector for some time and consider it one of the key megatrends or a secular growth sector, one that does not suffer from economic volatility. These services are a must in the era of the Internet and now AI, where everything is interconnected and susceptible to attack and disruption that can cost far more than prevention. Last year, I analyzed the Global X Cybersecurity ETF (BUG) and found it to be a solid methodology, but at that point, expensive, and I reduced it to a Hold. I am now updating the view and upgrading to a Buy with 24% upside potential to YE26.

Portfolio Holding Changes

BUG tracks the INDXX Cybersecurity Index, which selects a maximum of 40 stocks from a wide array of subsectors and from companies that need to have at least 50% of revenue derived from cybersecurity. Given that the ETF has 24 stocks, it appears that the listed companies in the sector are scarce. The index also has a maximum weight of 6% per stock, which is based on market cap adjusted for free float and is rebalanced and reconstituted twice a year in February and November.

The current portfolio is slated to be rebalanced with Zscaler (ZS), CrowdStrike (CRWD), Palo Alto (PANW), and CyberArk (CYBR) reducing weights and distributing to others. Probably, CyberArk will not be eliminated until the merger with Palo Alto is approved, and thus, this should remain the largest de facto position until mid-2026.

The largest changes were the addition of SailPoint (SAIL) and Rubrick (RBRK), with almost 5% each. As seen in the performance section, this did not work well.

Performance

Despite excellent gains from the top four holdings in the ETF, the new additions, SailPoint and Rubrick, plus Fortinet (FTNT), dragged down the year-to-date performance.

Consensus upside of 24% to YE26

I gathered consensus price targets for 94% of the fund's AUM and calculated a weighted average upside of 24%, which is quite high vs what was calculated a year ago at around 9%. This could outperform the market and has upside risk when the ETF is rebalanced, as more weight may be added to Check Point (CHKP), SailPoint, etc., that have higher price targets vs. the top three. CyberArk will trade in line with Palo Alto and may add a few points unless the deal falls through.

Growth and Margin

Using consensus estimates for the ETF holdings, I calculated revenue growth of over 13% for 2026, which is almost half the rate that the top weight may grow at and is impacted by the more mature companies. Adjusted Net Income (adding back stock-based compensation) margins are expected to increase substantially from 14% to 20% by 2027 as many smaller companies attain scale and cross over into positive margins.

This combination of good revenue growth with expanding margins may drive adjusted EPS by 63% in 2026. Note that many companies are transitioning from losses to gains, and that influences the growth rate. For 2027, this earnings growth begins to normalize at around 24%, still high.

Valuation

Based on the consensus estimates, the BUG portfolio does not appear expensive on a relative or PEG basis, trading at 0.7x for the 2026-2027 period. While the top-tier stocks are over 2x PEG, a sizable portion of the smaller companies are cheaper due to very rapid earnings growth and margin expansion, as discussed earlier. The sector is scaling rapidly, with many companies transitioning to positive margins vs losses, which is a multi-year phenomenon.

Risk

The cybersecurity stocks are volatile and impacted by short-term earnings results that may not meet the market's expectations. We have seen many 10% to 20% sell-downs on poor guidance, as well as the occasional accident where a company's software did not meet up to standards. In addition, cyberattack complexity may be augmented with AI, which requires constant innovation and R&D for security companies, where failure can lead to significant business damage.

Conclusion

I rate BUG a Buy. The pure-play cybersecurity ETF will probably continue to be volatile, but the diversification should mitigate long-lasting damage. While the smaller companies offer exceptional upside as they transition to positive margins, they are highly susceptible to setbacks and failure. At this point, BUG offers a good balance of risk and reward.

data.title: Strata Critical Medical: Beginning A New Journey
data.description:
Today, I'm taking a look at Strata Critical Medical (SRTA), which until recently was known as Blade Air Mobility, which I last profiled in June 2024. Management recently made a key divestiture to change the company's business model, which necessitated the name change to Strata Critical Medical. The firm also picked up a new business as well. An updated analysis around this transformed company follows below.

Company Overview:

Leadership disposed of its Blade Passenger business to Joby Aviation (JOBY) for up to $125 million. This restructure made Strata a more focused company and a pure-play medical logistics company where it transports organs to destinations around the country.

Strata Critical Medical will continue to rely on existing agreements with aircraft operators for dedicated medical transportation. In addition to some corporate efficiencies, this should transform Strata into being a more attractive growth play for shareholders over time.

The medical logistics business segment had grown from approximately 12% of overall revenues in 2020 to a current 60%. It also accounts for roughly 85% of adjusted EBITDA for the previously combined company. Management provided initial FY2025 sales guidance of $180 million to $190 million for the remaining business.

Then in mid-September, Strata announced the purchase of Keystone Perfusion Services for $124 million (roughly nine times EBITDA). The deal was for 88% cash and 12% stock. Keystone is a major player in organ recovery and regional perfusion services within the transplant sector. Keystone should deliver 50% sales growth in FY2025 to $65 million with adjusted EBITDA of $13 million. The equity now trades for $4.25 a share and sports a market capitalization of just north $380 million.

Third Quarter Results:

Strata Critical Medical posted Q3 results on Nov. 10. The company had a GAAP loss of 12 cents a share, a dime a share below expectations. GAAP net loss for the quarter of $9.7 million, compared to $5.6 million in the same quarter a year ago. The increase was partly due to integration costs associated with the recent Keystone acquisition. The bigger impact was driven by a $5.2 million increase in a realized loss from sales of short-term investments and a legal reserve in the current period. Adjusted EBITDA rose nearly 94% to $7.6 million. Not including Keystone, this metric rose 80%.

Revenues rose nearly 37% on a year-over-year basis to $49.3 million. This beat the consensus by north of $5 million. Standalone revenue growth was 29%, which doesn't incorporate the contribution from Keystone. Management boosted FY2025 sales guidance by $5 million to $185 million to $195 million.

Analyst Commentary and Balance Sheet:

Strata Critical Medical has a solid balance sheet with no debt and just over $75 million in cash and marketable securities based on the 10-K filing for Q3.

Just under 3% of the outstanding float in the shares is currently held short. Strata Critical Medical has a small Wall Street following. Only four analyst firms have chimed in around Strata so far this year. That said, they're uniformly bullish, as can be seen below.

Risks/Rewards off New Business Model:

Management's embrace of a new business model has notable benefits as well as a couple of risks. By moving to an "asset lite" model, Strata reduces its capital expenditure needs substantially as well as other costs such as staffing and maintenance associated with operating and maintaining a fleet of aircraft for passenger transportation purposes. That business has been sold to Joby, as previously noted.

This should have no impact on customer relationships as far as it pertains to organ transport. Strata will remain a key player in the organ transplant ecosystem, concentrating on the organ transport logistics part of the equation, as well as now being able to offer perfusion services to customers.

Strata should continue to benefit from trends in organ transplant transportation (above), which favor larger players compared to local operations and non-profits. Key reasons this part of the business had quintupled its contribution to Blade's overall revenues since 2020, before the recent business divestiture.

A key risk is ensuring the perfusion business recently acquired by the purchase of Keystone is seamlessly integrated into Strata's overall operational processes and overall offerings. Leadership raising FY2025 guidance after Q3 results and producing operational cash flow on this front during the quarter is encouraging. Management has also stated it expects the company to consistently generate free cash flow moving forward.

The other risk is it might take a few quarters of operations under the new restructure to produce the necessary data points for investors and analyst firms to become more enthusiastic about Strata's new business model as well as its stock. Currently the company has little coverage on Wall Street, hopefully something that changes in the coming year.

Conclusion:

The one analyst firm that has posted projections sees revenues falling 23% this year with the divestiture of the passenger business. The two analyst firms that have projections out for FY2026 see sales moving to $260 million in FY2026. No analyst firms seemed to have put a target on earnings at this time.

SRTA is currently trading at under 1.5x FY2026E revenues, considerably cheaper accounting for the net cash on the company's balance sheet. Much larger TransMedics Group, Inc. (TMDX), which is also involved in the organ transplant sector, is valued at approximately 5.4x FY2026E sales.

It's admittedly an imperfect comparison. However, it does point to the potential of the shares in a growing industry. Therefore, the shares merit a small speculative holding while awaiting further data points as Strata Critical Medical embarks on its new journey.
